overview.argument: Romans is the fullest, most ordered statement of the gospel in the New Testament — a letter written to introduce Paul and his message to a church he did not found. Its thesis is stated at the outset: the gospel is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes, for in it the righteousness of God is revealed from faith to faith. From there the argument marches: first the whole world is shown guilty — Gentile (1:18–32) and Jew (2:1–3:20) alike are under sin; then God's answer, a righteousness apart from law, given freely to all who believe and grounded in Christ's atoning death (3:21–5:21); then the new life of those united to Christ — dead to sin, freed from the law's condemnation, led by the Spirit, and secured in an unbreakable love (chs. 6–8). Paul then wrestles with the place of Israel in God's sovereign purpose (chs. 9–11), and turns the whole to the shape of a transformed life in worship, love, citizenship, and forbearance (12:1–15:13), closing with his travel plans and a long roll of greetings.
overview.occasion: Paul writes from Corinth about AD 57, near the close of his third missionary journey, as he prepares to carry the Gentile churches' collection to Jerusalem and then, he hopes, to visit Rome on his way to Spain (15:23–28). The Roman church — a mixed body of Jewish and Gentile believers he had neither founded nor yet seen — is both his intended partner for the westward mission and a congregation whose Jew–Gentile tensions the letter addresses. So Romans is at once a full setting-out of Paul's gospel and a pastoral word to hold that church together in Christ.
overview.theology: Romans is the seedbed of Christian theology. Here are the classic doctrines in their sharpest biblical form: universal sin and the fall in Adam (5:12–21); justification by grace through faith, apart from works of the law (3:28); Christ as the hilastērion, the propitiation whose blood both justifies the sinner and vindicates the righteousness of God (3:25–26); union with Christ in his death and resurrection (6:5); life in the Spirit and the assurance that nothing can separate the believer from God's love (8:38–39); and the sovereign electing mercy of God, held together with Israel's future (11:26). It was Romans that converted Augustine, that lit the Reformation for Luther over the phrase "the righteous shall live by faith," and that has reordered the church's understanding of grace in every age since.
overview.christ: The letter is "the gospel of God … concerning his Son" (1:1–4). Christ is the descendant of David declared Son of God in power by the resurrection; the propitiation set forth in his blood (3:25); the last Adam whose one act of obedience undoes the ruin of the first (5:19); the Lord with whom the believer dies and rises (6:4); the end of the law for righteousness to everyone who believes (10:4); and the risen Lord who intercedes at God's right hand (8:34). To confess him as Lord and believe God raised him is to be saved (10:9).

structure.label: The gospel of God — the righteousness of God by faith
structure.gist: Greeting, longing to visit, and the letter's thesis: from faith to faith.

structure.label: The whole world guilty before God
structure.gist: Gentile and Jew alike are under sin; no one is righteous by law.

structure.label: Justified by faith — righteousness apart from law
structure.gist: The propitiation in Christ, Abraham's faith, peace with God, Adam and Christ.

structure.label: Free from sin, law, and death — life in the Spirit
structure.gist: United to Christ, freed from the law, no condemnation, more than conquerors.

structure.label: God's sovereign purpose and the future of Israel
structure.gist: Election and mercy, the remnant, the ingrafted Gentiles, and all Israel.

structure.label: The living sacrifice — the gospel shaping life
structure.gist: Renewed minds, sincere love, the governing authorities, the weak and the strong.

structure.label: Paul's ministry, plans, and greetings
structure.gist: The apostle to the Gentiles, travel plans, a long roll of names, closing doxology.

external.name: Douglas J. Moo
external.work: The Letter to the Romans (NICNT, 2nd ed.)
external.note: Reads the letter around the revelation of God's saving righteousness and the salvation-historical shift from law to Christ.

external.name: C.E.B. Cranfield
external.work: The Epistle to the Romans (ICC, 2 vols)
external.note: The exacting exegetical standard; defends a robust reading of justification and the law within a Reformed frame.

external.name: James D.G. Dunn
external.note: Frames "works of the law" within the New Perspective — covenant boundary-markers as much as legalism.

external.name: Leon Morris
external.work: The Epistle to the Romans (Pillar NT Commentary)
external.note: Keeps propitiation and the wrath-averting blood of Christ at the letter's center.

external.name: Thomas R. Schreiner
external.note: A Reformed reading emphasizing God's righteousness, sovereign grace, and the obedience of faith.

external.name: N.T. Wright
external.work: The Letter to the Romans (New Interpreter's Bible)
external.note: Reads Romans as the story of God's covenant faithfulness reaching its climax in the Messiah and the renewed people.
